stories.title: Amazon Q Developer agentic capabilities on SWE-Bench
stories.summary: Amazon Q Developer's agentic capabilities have claimed top positions on both SWE-Bench and SWE-Bench Lite leaderboards, which evaluate AI systems on real GitHub issues requiring multi-file edits, test writing, and code review across open-source Python repositories. SWE-Bench measures an agent's ability to resolve actual filed issues — not synthetic prompts — by applying patches that pass the associated test suites, making it a more grounded benchmark than most. The leaderboard scores reflect performance on tasks like locating the relevant files, understanding cross-module dependencies, writing regression tests, and producing a correct diff. Practically, this means Q Developer's agent can now be pointed at a filed bug or feature request and has a benchmark-verified higher probability of producing a working, test-passing patch than competing agents.
stories.considerations: The concrete scenario where this matters: you're triaging a backlog of 40 GitHub issues in a mid-sized Python service — Q Developer's agent can autonomously attempt resolution, run tests, and surface a diff for review, compressing what might be a 2-hour triage session into a review-only workflow. The non-obvious implication is that SWE-Bench performance correlates heavily with how well an agent navigates repo structure and reads existing test patterns, so Q's score suggests its file-traversal and context-retrieval strategy is meaningfully better — not just its code generation. This matters now because agentic coding tools have hit a credibility wall: most teams have tried them and found they break on anything beyond single-file edits, so a verifiable multi-step benchmark result directly addresses that skepticism. The honest gotcha: SWE-Bench is Python-only and skews toward well-tested open-source repos with clear issue descriptions — performance on your internal Java monorepo with sparse tests and ambiguous tickets will almost certainly be lower, so treat the benchmark as a ceiling, not a baseline.

stories.title: Composer 2.5 Model Release
stories.summary: Cursor released Composer 2.5, an updated agentic coding model replacing Composer 2. The model ships in two tiers: Standard at $0.50/M input and $2.50/M output tokens, and Fast at $3.00/M input and $15.00/M output tokens — roughly a 6x price gap between tiers, suggesting meaningfully different inference infrastructure. The changelog claims improvements in sustained multi-step task execution and instruction-following fidelity, though no benchmark numbers or context-window specs are published. Developers can now select 2.5 as their Composer model for long-running agentic sessions where 2.0 was losing coherence or drifting from instructions mid-task.
stories.considerations: The most concrete use case is multi-file refactors — say, migrating a 50+ file Express codebase to a new middleware pattern — where earlier Composer versions would gradually ignore constraints like 'don't modify test files' or 'preserve existing error handling.' The 6x price differential between Standard and Fast tiers implies these aren't just speed variants but likely different model sizes or serving strategies, meaning you may get qualitatively different outputs, not just latency differences — worth testing before committing Fast tier to production pipelines. This release directly addresses the most common Composer complaint: instruction drift on tasks exceeding 10-15 tool calls, which has been a recurring thread in Cursor's Discord and forums. The gotcha: 'more pleasant to collaborate with' is doing a lot of work in this changelog — there are zero hard metrics, no context window disclosure, and no evals shared, so you're flying blind on whether 2.5 actually outperforms 2.0 on your specific task distribution until you run your own tests.

stories.title: Claude Opus 4.7 fast mode now available
stories.summary: Windsurf has added a 'fast mode' variant of Claude Opus 4.7 to its platform, delivering approximately 2.5x higher token output speeds compared to the standard Opus 4.7 mode. The speed increase likely comes from infrastructure-level optimizations — speculative decoding, batching, or dedicated capacity — rather than model quantization or capability reduction, since Windsurf explicitly claims full model parity. Previously, using Opus 4.7 in Windsurf meant accepting standard inference latency; now developers can run the same model at roughly 2.5x throughput for code generation and analysis tasks.
stories.considerations: In a tight edit-test-fix loop on a large TypeScript monorepo, the difference between waiting 8 seconds versus 3 seconds per Opus response compounds quickly across dozens of iterations — this is where 2.5x throughput actually changes whether you stay in flow or context-switch. The non-obvious implication: if fast mode is priced the same as standard, it will likely become the default choice for all interactive use cases, effectively deprecating standard mode in practice even if it remains available. This matters now because Opus-class models have historically been penalized by latency in agentic workflows where multiple sequential calls are required — 2.5x speed makes multi-step code agents meaningfully more usable. The honest gotcha: 'approximately 2.5x' and 'without sacrificing capability' are marketing claims with no published benchmark backing — developers should run their own evals on complex reasoning tasks like multi-file refactors before assuming full parity, since infrastructure optimizations sometimes introduce subtle consistency tradeoffs under load.

stories.title: Filter session history by workspace directory
stories.summary: Continue.dev added workspace-directory filtering to session history, letting users scope the chat history panel to show only sessions initiated from a specific folder. The filter works by tagging each session with its originating workspace path at creation time and then applying a directory-match predicate when rendering the history list. No context-window or API changes are involved — this is purely a UI/navigation feature in the sidebar. Developers who maintain separate repos for frontend, backend, and infra can now pull up only the conversations relevant to the repo they have open, without scrolling past unrelated sessions.
stories.considerations: If you're context-switching between, say, a monorepo and three microservice repos daily, this directly cuts the time spent hunting for a specific debugging session — previously you'd scroll through a flat global list mixing conversations from all projects. The non-obvious implication: session history is now implicitly tied to filesystem paths, which means renaming or moving a project directory will likely orphan those sessions from the filter, making them invisible unless you switch back to the global view. This lands at a moment when AI-assisted coding sessions are getting longer and more numerous — the global history list was becoming genuinely unwieldy for anyone running Continue across more than two or three active projects. Worth noting that this is a client-side filter, not a separate storage partition, so all sessions still accumulate in the same store — if you're hoping for actual isolation or export-per-project, that's not what this is.

stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Synchronous Middleware for Every Tool Call
stories.summary: Claude Code now ships a hooks system that injects user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts at 25+ named lifecycle points in the agentic loop — SessionStart, PreToolUse/PostToolUse pairs for every tool, and Stop, among others. Each hook receives full JSON context on stdin describing the pending tool call and returns a structured decision (allow, block, or modify) that Claude Code enforces synchronously before execution proceeds. The critical mechanism: exit code 2 causes the hook's stdout to be injected directly into Claude's context as an error message, meaning a 10-line bash script can act as a blocking linter that Claude must satisfy before touching the filesystem. This lets developers enforce invariants — schema validation, secret scanning, test-pass requirements, audit logging — that were previously impossible to guarantee when Claude operated autonomously across large codebases.
stories.considerations: The most concrete win: in a 300-file TypeScript monorepo mid-refactor, a PreToolUse hook on Write/Edit tools can run tsc --noEmit against the staged change and return exit code 2 with the compiler error if types break — Claude then self-corrects before the bad write ever lands, eliminating the 'silent drift then bulk fix' cycle that plagues long agentic sessions. Second-order effect worth thinking about: hooks run synchronously in the loop, so a slow hook (say, an LLM-based policy checker hitting an external API) directly multiplies latency across every tool call — a 500ms hook on a 200-tool session adds 100 seconds of wall time, which will surprise teams that reach for heavy validators. This matters now because the main blocker to trusting Claude Code on production repos has been the lack of a hard enforcement layer — guardrails were advisory at best, and post-hoc review of diffs doesn't catch cascading side effects mid-session. The gotcha: hooks are defined in project or user config and run with the same OS permissions as Claude Code itself, so a misconfigured hook that always exits 0 silently becomes a no-op guardrail — teams need to test hook failure paths explicitly, not just the happy path.
